description:
You are writing a small certificate–maintenance utility for a
        colleague who needs a quick way to keep track of certificate
        expiration dates.

        1.  Create a new directory  
            /home/user/cert_utils  
            with permissions 755.

        2.  Inside that directory write an executable POSIX-shell script
            named  check_certs.sh  that behaves exactly as follows:

            • Invocation syntax  
              ./check_certs.sh [PATH]  

              - If the optional PATH argument is omitted the script shall
                use  /home/user/certs  as its working directory.  
              - The script must examine **every file that ends in
                “.pem”** in the target directory.

            • Parallelism  
              It must process the *.pem files with **no more than four
              parallel background jobs** (e.g., with GNU parallel,
              xargs -P, or manual backgrounding & wait logic).  
              Any approach is acceptable as long as at most four
              certificates are inspected concurrently.

            • Data extraction  
              Each *.pem file is guaranteed to contain a line whose
              literal prefix is  
              “Not After : ” (note the two spaces after the colon).  
              The script shall read that line, strip everything except
              the ISO-8601 date portion  YYYY-MM-DD, and remember the
              filename and that date.

            • Output  
              After all files are processed the script shall create/
              overwrite the file  
              /home/user/cert_utils/cert_report.json  
              containing a single JSON array sorted **alphabetically by
              filename**.  The array elements must look exactly like
              this (no extra keys, no pretty-printing differences):

                   {
                     "file": "<filename>",
                     "expiration_date": "<YYYY-MM-DD>"
                   }

              Example for one element (spacing and newlines must match):

                   {
                     "file": "example.pem",
                     "expiration_date": "2029-04-01"
                   }

              The final file must therefore be identical byte-for-byte
              to what an automated test will expect, apart from the two
              known certificate names.

        3.  Run the script once (letting it default to
            /home/user/certs).  
            Successful execution should leave exactly one new file
            called  cert_report.json  in /home/user/cert_utils.

        4.  Create a short hand-written documentation file
            /home/user/cert_utils/README.txt  that:

            • explains in 3–6 concise lines how the script works,  
            • mentions the need for a maximum of four parallel jobs, and  
            • ends with the *literal* marker line  

                  ##EOF

        5.  Display the contents of cert_report.json on the console so
            the user can visually confirm the output.

        Notes & verification hints
        • Do NOT hard-code the certificate list; discover *.pem files at
          runtime.  
        • JSON must contain exactly two objects (alpha.pem and
          beta.pem) and nothing else.  
        • The order **must be alphabetical**.  
        • Whitespace and newlines must match the example style because
          the grader performs a strict byte-level comparison.
truth:
Pre-existing artefacts before the agent starts
        ------------------------------------------------
        Directory:
            /home/user/certs            (already created, 755)

        Files inside /home/user/certs (permissions 644):

        1. /home/user/certs/alpha.pem
           Contents (exact, including blank lines):

-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
Dummy certificate placeholder only for testing utilities.
Serial Number: 01
Not After : 2030-12-31 23:59:59 GMT
Subject CN=alpha.example.io
-----END CERTIFICATE-----

        2. /home/user/certs/beta.pem
           Contents (exact):

-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
Dummy certificate placeholder only for testing utilities.
Serial Number: 02
Not After : 2025-06-15 12:00:00 GMT
Subject CN=beta.example.io
-----END CERTIFICATE-----

        Expected artefacts after correct task completion
        ------------------------------------------------

        1. Directory /home/user/cert_utils       (755)

        2. Executable script /home/user/cert_utils/check_certs.sh
           – file is executable (755 or 750)
           – script uses a maximum of four concurrent processes
             to parse files.
           – no hard-coded certificate names.

        3. JSON report /home/user/cert_utils/cert_report.json
           Exact byte-for-byte contents:

[
  {
    "file": "alpha.pem",
    "expiration_date": "2030-12-31"
  },
  {
    "file": "beta.pem",
    "expiration_date": "2025-06-15"
  }
]

        4. Documentation file /home/user/cert_utils/README.txt
           – 3–6 non-empty lines of explanatory text  
           – last line is literally “##EOF”

        5. The agent will have displayed the content of
           cert_report.json to stdout at least once.
